front: A veteran officer watches two men repeatedly walk past a store window and confer, suspects a casing for a daylight robbery, stops them, and pats down their outer clothing — finding a pistol. The Court approves the stop and the weapons pat-down on less than probable cause. Which case?
back:
**Terry v. Ohio (1968)**
Holding: On reasonable, articulable suspicion that criminal activity is afoot an officer may make a brief investigative stop, and on RAS that the person is armed and presently dangerous may conduct a limited protective frisk of the outer clothing for weapons.
Signature: "specific and articulable facts" — more than a hunch, less than probable cause; the frisk is for weapons only.
Why it matters: the foundation of stop-and-frisk; two SEPARATE showings — RAS-to-stop (crime afoot) and RAS-to-frisk (armed & dangerous).
⚠ Don't confuse with **Robinson** SIA (a full search of the arrestee) — a Terry frisk is a weapons pat-down only, on RAS, with no arrest.

front: An officer who has watched a man talk to known addicts tells him "You know what I am after," then thrusts his hand straight into the man's pocket and pulls out heroin — no initial pat for weapons. The Court suppresses the drugs. Which case?
back:
**Sibron v. New York (1968)**
Holding: A frisk requires RAS that the person is armed and dangerous, and is limited to a pat-down for weapons; reaching directly into a pocket for narcotics is not a protective frisk and is unlawful.
Signature: the officer "thrust his hand into Sibron's pocket" looking for narcotics, not weapons — scope unrelated to officer safety.
Why it matters: Terry's companion case; the frisk is for WEAPONS, not evidence, and the search must match its protective justification.
⚠ Don't confuse with **Terry** (a proper weapons pat-down on RAS armed & dangerous) — Sibron is the same day's counter-example of an unlawful evidence-hunting search.

front: A known informant walks up to an officer and says a man in a nearby car is carrying narcotics and has a gun at his waist. The officer reaches in and pulls a loaded revolver from exactly that spot. The Court upholds the frisk on the tip. Which case?
back:
**Adams v. Williams (1972)**
Holding: Reasonable suspicion (and a frisk) may rest on a reliable informant's tip, not only the officer's own first-hand observation.
Signature: rejected the argument that RAS "can only be based on the officer's personal observation."
Why it matters: opens the door to tip-based RAS; the reliable-informant anchor of the anonymous-tip spectrum.
⚠ Don't confuse with **Florida v. J.L.** (a BARE anonymous tip that a person has a gun is NOT enough) — Adams involved a known, accountable informant.

front: During a lawful weapons pat-down the officer feels a small lump, decides it's not a weapon, but keeps squeezing and sliding it between his fingers until he concludes it's crack cocaine, then seizes it. The Court suppresses it. Which case?
back:
**Minnesota v. Dickerson (1993)**
Holding: "Plain feel" — if during a lawful Terry frisk an object's incriminating character is immediately apparent by touch, it may be seized; but contraband found only through further squeezing/manipulation beyond the weapons search is unlawful.
Signature: an object "whose contour or mass makes its identity immediately apparent" may be seized — here it was not, so the seizure failed.
Why it matters: the tactile analog of plain view; defines what a Terry frisk may lawfully seize.
⚠ Don't confuse with **Arizona v. Hicks** (moving stereo gear to read serial numbers) — same flaw: incriminating nature was NOT immediately apparent without a further search.

front: Officers stop a driver who has swerved into a ditch, see a hunting knife on the floorboard, and — without arresting him — search the passenger compartment for weapons, finding marijuana. The Court extends Terry's frisk rationale to the car's interior. Which case?
back:
**Michigan v. Long (1983)**
Holding: On specific, articulable facts giving RAS the suspect is dangerous and may gain immediate control of weapons, police may conduct a protective search of the passenger compartment limited to areas where a weapon may be placed or hidden — a "Terry frisk" of the car.
Signature: the protective sweep is scope-limited to where a weapon could be; no arrest required.
Why it matters: applies Terry's frisk to vehicles; distinct from a search incident to arrest.
⚠ Don't confuse with **Belton/Gant** vehicle SITA (requires an ARREST; broader scope) — Long is a weapons frisk of the car on RAS, no arrest needed.

front: A man holding a bag stands near a building in a known heavy-narcotics area, sees a four-car police caravan, and bolts — unprovoked headlong flight. Officers chase, catch him, and frisk out a handgun. The Court finds RAS. Which case?
back:
**Illinois v. Wardlow (2000)**
Holding: Unprovoked headlong flight upon noticing police, combined with presence in a high-crime area, can furnish reasonable suspicion for a Terry stop.
Signature: "Headlong flight — wherever it occurs — is the consummate act of evasion."
Why it matters: flight is a permissible RAS factor; but it was flight PLUS the high-crime context, not either alone.
⚠ Don't confuse with **Florida v. J.L.** (a bare anonymous gun tip, no flight, no corroboration — NOT enough). High-crime area alone is also not enough.

front: An anonymous caller predicts a woman will leave a particular apartment at a particular time, drive a brown Plymouth station wagon to a named motel, carrying cocaine. Police watch it all unfold as predicted, then stop her. The Court calls it a "close case" but upholds the stop. Which case?
back:
**Alabama v. White (1990)**
Holding: An anonymous tip can supply reasonable suspicion when sufficiently corroborated by police observation — especially of the tipster's prediction of future behavior, which shows inside knowledge.
Signature: "a close case," but the corroborated prediction gave the tip adequate indicia of reliability.
Why it matters: the middle of the tip spectrum — predictive corroboration can rescue an anonymous tip.
⚠ Don't confuse with **Florida v. J.L.** (bare tip, no predictive detail — NOT enough) or **Navarette** (a 911 caller reporting reckless driving — contemporaneous eyewitness reliability).

front: An anonymous caller says only that a young Black male in a plaid shirt at a particular bus stop is carrying a gun. Police go, find a matching youth, frisk him, and find a gun — but the only thing corroborated was his location and appearance. The Court suppresses it. Which case?
back:
**Florida v. J.L. (2000)**
Holding: A bare anonymous tip that a person is carrying a gun, without more, is NOT reasonable suspicion for a stop and frisk; an accurate description of a readily observable location and appearance does not show knowledge of concealed criminal activity.
Signature: no "firearm exception" to the reliability requirement — the tip must be reliable in its assertion of illegality, not just identification.
Why it matters: the floor of the anonymous-tip spectrum — bare tips fail.
⚠ Don't confuse with **Alabama v. White** (corroborated PREDICTION) or **Navarette** (contemporaneous 911 eyewitness of reckless driving). J.L. had neither.

front: A 911 caller reports that a silver Ford pickup just ran her off the road, giving the plate and location. Police find the truck, follow it, and stop it without seeing any bad driving themselves — finding 30 pounds of marijuana. The Court upholds the stop on the call. Which case?
back:
**Navarette v. California (2014)**
Holding: A 911 call reporting dangerous/reckless driving can supply reasonable suspicion for a stop when it bears adequate indicia of reliability — an eyewitness, contemporaneous report, on a recorded/traceable 911 system.
Signature: "the call bore adequate indicia of reliability for the officer to credit the caller's account."
Why it matters: a reliable 911 tip can justify a stop even without the officer's own corroboration of the offense.
⚠ Don't confuse with **Florida v. J.L.** (bare, untraceable tip — fails) — the 911 system's traceability and the contemporaneous eyewitness account supplied reliability here.

front: A traveler pays $2,100 cash for tickets from a roll of twenties, flies under a name not matching his phone, takes a brief trip to Miami, appears nervous, and checks no luggage — each fact innocent alone. The Court says together they are RAS. Which case?
back:
**United States v. Sokolow (1989)**
Holding: Factors each individually consistent with innocent travel (a drug-courier profile) can, taken together, amount to reasonable suspicion.
Signature: "Any one of these factors is not by itself proof of any illegal conduct ... but ... taken together they amount to reasonable suspicion."
Why it matters: validates the totality approach and profile-type factors — innocence of each piece doesn't defeat RAS of the whole.
⚠ Don't confuse with **Arvizu** (the anti-"divide-and-conquer" command) — same totality theme, different angle: Sokolow says innocent factors can combine; Arvizu forbids parsing them in isolation.

front: A border agent stops a minivan on a remote smuggling route — a stiff wave, kids' knees propped oddly high, a registered-to-a-smuggling-area plate. The Court of Appeals dissected each factor away; SCOTUS reverses, condemning that approach. Which case?
back:
**United States v. Arvizu (2002)**
Holding: Reasonable suspicion is judged on the totality of the circumstances — the "whole picture" — and reviewing courts may NOT use a "divide-and-conquer" analysis that evaluates and discards each factor in isolation.
Signature: "Terry ... precludes this sort of divide-and-conquer analysis."
Why it matters: locks in deferential, holistic RAS review crediting an officer's experience.
⚠ Don't confuse with **Sokolow** (innocent factors can combine into RAS) — Arvizu is the procedural twin: don't parse the factors apart.

front: Officers deduce from tracks in the desert and a pattern of crossings that a particular vehicle ("Chevron") will be carrying smuggled aliens on a given night, and stop it. Which case gave us the "particularized and objective basis" / "whole picture" formulation of reasonable suspicion?
back:
**United States v. Cortez (1981)**
Holding: Reasonable suspicion requires a particularized and objective basis for suspecting the particular person stopped, assessed on the totality of the circumstances — the "whole picture."
Signature: "the totality of the circumstances — the whole picture — must be taken into account ... a particularized and objective basis."
Why it matters: the canonical definition of RAS, quoted in nearly every later stop case.
⚠ Don't confuse with **Terry** (the original "specific and articulable facts" frisk case) — Cortez supplies the totality/"whole picture" phrasing the Court now repeats.

front: Police in one town issue a "wanted" flyer naming a suspect in a completed armed robbery. Officers in another town, relying on the flyer, make a Terry stop — not to prevent an ongoing crime but to investigate a past one. The Court upholds the stop. Which case?
back:
**United States v. Hensley (1985)**
Holding: A Terry stop may be made on reasonable suspicion, grounded in specific and articulable facts, that a person was involved in or is wanted for a COMPLETED felony — and officers may rely on a wanted flyer/bulletin issued on RAS.
Signature: extended Terry from crime-prevention to investigating past crimes; the "wanted flyer" / collective-knowledge stop.
Why it matters: Terry is not limited to imminent/ongoing offenses; a flyer issued on RAS justifies the stop by the acting officer.
⚠ Don't confuse with **Terry/Adams** (suspicion of ongoing criminal activity) — Hensley authorizes stops to investigate already-completed felonies.

front: An officer runs a truck's plate, learns the registered owner's license is revoked, and — without seeing the driver or any violation — stops the truck on the inference the owner is driving. The Court upholds it. Which case?
back:
**Kansas v. Glover (2020)**
Holding: When an officer lacks information negating the inference that the registered owner is the driver, reasonable suspicion may rest on the common-sense inference that the owner (whose license is revoked) is behind the wheel.
Signature: RAS may rest on a common-sense inference, not just direct observation — "the stop is reasonable."
Why it matters: officers may draw reasonable probabilistic inferences for RAS; the inference dissolves if facts on hand negate it (e.g., driver visibly doesn't match the owner).
⚠ Don't confuse with **Delaware v. Prouse** (a RANDOM, suspicionless plate/license check is unconstitutional) — Glover had a specific, individualized inference of a violation.

front: An officer stops a car for a single broken brake light, mistakenly believing state law requires two working ones (it doesn't). The mistake of law was reasonable, and the Court upholds the stop and the cocaine found on consent. Which case?
back:
**Heien v. North Carolina (2014)**
Holding: A traffic stop can rest on an officer's objectively reasonable mistake of law (as well as a reasonable mistake of fact); a reasonable legal error can supply the reasonable suspicion for the stop.
Signature: no reason a reasonable mistake of fact counts "but not ... a similarly reasonable mistake of law."
Why it matters: cabined — the mistake must itself be OBJECTIVELY reasonable; ignorance of clear, settled law does not qualify.
⚠ Don't confuse with **Whren** (subjective MOTIVE is irrelevant if there's an objective violation) — Heien is about a reasonable MISTAKE, not pretext.

front: An officer finishes writing a warning ticket, then makes the driver wait 7–8 extra minutes for a drug dog to arrive and sniff the car — the ticket business already done. The Court holds the added time unlawful. Which case?
back:
**Rodriguez v. United States (2015)**
Holding: A traffic stop may last no longer than needed to complete its "mission"; absent independent reasonable suspicion, police may not prolong it — even briefly — for an unrelated dog sniff after the mission is done.
Signature: the seizure "become[s] unlawful if it is prolonged beyond the time reasonably required to complete th[e] mission"; the measure is diligence.
Why it matters: kills the "de minimis extension" idea — adding time off-mission needs its own RAS; multitasking that adds no time is fine.
⚠ Don't confuse with **Caballes** (a dog sniff DURING a lawful, unprolonged stop is not a search) — Rodriguez is about ADDING time after the mission ends.

front: Agents detain a suspected drug-running pickup and its companion car for about 20 minutes while they sort out the convoy and confirm suspicions. The defendant argues the stop was too long to be a Terry stop. The Court disagrees — there's no rigid time limit. Which case?
back:
**United States v. Sharpe (1985)**
Holding: There is no rigid time limit on an investigative stop; the question is whether police diligently pursued a means of investigation likely to confirm or dispel their suspicion quickly. A roughly 20-minute stop was reasonable.
Signature: examine "whether the police diligently pursued [the] investigation" — diligence, not a stopwatch.
Why it matters: duration is a diligence test; "it took X minutes, therefore unlawful" is not the standard.
⚠ Don't confuse with **Royer** (the detention became too INTRUSIVE — moving him to a small room, holding his ticket/luggage — ripening into a de facto arrest) — Sharpe is about DURATION, judged by diligence.

front: Detectives flag a "drug-courier-profile" airline passenger, keep his ticket and ID, move him to a small police room, retrieve his checked bags, and get "consent" to open them — finding marijuana. The Court says the detention had become a de facto arrest. Which case?
back:
**Florida v. Royer (1983)**
Holding: An investigative detention must use the least intrusive means reasonably available and stay within Terry's limits; when it becomes too intrusive (holding ID, moving the suspect, retaining luggage) it ripens into a de facto arrest requiring probable cause — here absent, so the "consent" was tainted.
Signature: a Terry stop's scope must be "carefully tailored"; over-intrusive detention swallows the rule that seizures need probable cause.
Why it matters: defines when an investigative stop crosses into an arrest — a scope/intrusiveness limit (not a clock).
⚠ Don't confuse with **Sharpe** (the issue was DURATION — ~20 min OK on diligence) — Royer turned on INTRUSIVENESS, not length.

front: During a valid Terry stop on a report of an assault, an officer asks a man for his name eleven times under a state stop-and-identify statute; the man refuses and is arrested for that refusal. The Court upholds the conviction. Which case?
back:
**Hiibel v. Sixth Judicial District Court (2004)**
Holding: A state stop-and-identify statute that requires a suspect to disclose his NAME during a valid Terry stop is consistent with the Fourth Amendment (and does not violate the Fifth on these facts).
Signature: "A state law requiring a suspect to disclose his name in the course of a valid Terry stop is consistent with Fourth Amendment prohibitions."
Why it matters: a name may be compelled during a lawful stop — but the STOP must be valid first, and the statute reaches only the name.
⚠ Don't confuse with **Brown v. Texas** (no valid RAS for the stop — so you CANNOT be punished for refusing to identify). Hiibel = valid stop; Brown = invalid stop.
cite: Hiibel v. Sixth Judicial Dist. Court of Nev., 542 U.S. 177, 188 (2004)

front: Officers in an alley in a high-drug area see a man who "looked suspicious," stop him with no articulable facts, demand he identify himself, and arrest him under a stop-and-identify statute when he refuses. The Court reverses the conviction. Which case?
back:
**Brown v. Texas (1979)**
Holding: A person cannot be detained and required to identify himself — or punished for refusing — absent reasonable suspicion that he is or has been engaged in criminal conduct. "Looked suspicious" with no supporting facts is not RAS.
Signature: the detention and demand for ID "violated the Fourth Amendment because the officers lacked any reasonable suspicion."
Why it matters: a stop-and-identify demand is only as good as the RAS underlying the stop; no RAS, no lawful demand.
⚠ Don't confuse with **Hiibel** (a VALID Terry stop — so compelling a name was OK). Brown had NO valid stop, so the ID demand failed.

front: Officers stop a car for an expired plate and, as a matter of routine, order the DRIVER to step out; as he does, they spot a bulge and frisk a loaded revolver. The Court approves the order-out as a matter of course. Which case?
back:
**Pennsylvania v. Mimms (1977)**
Holding: Once a vehicle is lawfully stopped for a traffic violation, an officer may order the DRIVER out of the car as a matter of course; the marginal intrusion is outweighed by officer safety. No separate suspicion needed for the order-out.
Signature: order the driver out "without violating the Fourth Amendment."
Why it matters: ordering occupants out is a control measure, not a search — needs no suspicion (the later FRISK still needs RAS armed & dangerous).
⚠ Don't confuse with **Maryland v. Wilson** (extends order-out to PASSENGERS) or **Arizona v. Johnson** (FRISKING a passenger needs RAS armed & dangerous).

front: On a speeding stop the officer notices a nervous front-seat PASSENGER and orders him out of the car; as he steps out, crack cocaine drops to the ground. The Court extends the order-out rule from drivers to passengers. Which case?
back:
**Maryland v. Wilson (1997)**
Holding: An officer making a lawful traffic stop may order the PASSENGERS, as well as the driver, out of the car pending completion of the stop — again as a control measure needing no separate suspicion.
Signature: "an officer making a traffic stop may order passengers to get out of the car pending completion of the stop."
Why it matters: extends Mimms to passengers; the order-out (no suspicion) is distinct from a frisk (RAS required).
⚠ Don't confuse with **Mimms** (the DRIVER) or **Arizona v. Johnson** (FRISKING a passenger needs RAS armed & dangerous). Order out = no suspicion; pat down = RAS.

front: During a traffic stop for suspended registration, an officer who suspects a back-seat PASSENGER is armed (gang attire, a scanner, prison talk) pats him down and finds a gun — even though she has no suspicion he committed any crime. The Court upholds the frisk. Which case?
back:
**Arizona v. Johnson (2009)**
Holding: An officer may frisk a passenger during a lawful traffic stop on reasonable suspicion that the passenger is armed and dangerous — no separate suspicion that the passenger is engaged in criminal activity is required.
Signature: "the police must harbor reasonable suspicion that the person subjected to the frisk is armed and dangerous."
Why it matters: completes the traffic triad — order out (no suspicion) vs. frisk the person (RAS armed) vs. frisk the car (RAS of a weapon, Long).
⚠ Don't confuse with **Wilson/Mimms** (ordering occupants OUT needs no suspicion) — Johnson's PAT-DOWN requires RAS armed & dangerous.

front: A patrolman stops a car at random — no traffic violation, no suspicion, just to check the license and registration — and smells marijuana. The Court holds these discretionary, standardless spot checks unconstitutional. Which case?
back:
**Delaware v. Prouse (1979)**
Holding: Random, suspicionless stops of motorists to check license and registration are unreasonable; an officer needs at least articulable and reasonable suspicion of a violation. (Neutral, systematic checkpoints are not foreclosed.)
Signature: standardless, discretionary spot stops fail; the RAS floor for discretionary vehicle stops.
Why it matters: establishes that a traffic stop needs RAS (or PC); set up the checkpoint cases that allow suspicionless but systematic stops.
⚠ Don't confuse with **Martinez-Fuerte / Sitz** (FIXED, neutral checkpoints — no individualized suspicion needed) — Prouse bans ROVING discretionary stops, not systematic checkpoints.

front: A roving Border Patrol crew on a highway near the border stops a car solely because its occupants "appear to be of Mexican ancestry" and questions them about immigration status. The Court holds that single factor cannot justify the stop. Which case?
back:
**United States v. Brignoni-Ponce (1975)**
Holding: A roving Border Patrol may stop a vehicle near the border to question occupants only on reasonable suspicion, based on specific articulable facts, that they are aliens illegally in the country; apparent Mexican ancestry ALONE is not enough.
Signature: "this factor alone would justify neither a reasonable belief that they were aliens, nor ... that the car concealed other aliens."
Why it matters: applies Terry's reasonable-suspicion standard to roving immigration stops; ancestry may be one relevant factor but never the sole basis.
⚠ Don't confuse with **Martinez-Fuerte** (FIXED interior immigration checkpoints — brief stops with NO individualized suspicion). Roving patrols need RAS; fixed checkpoints don't.
